summary: This guest post announces a new speaker series on Mathematics and Democracy, covering topics like election theory, redistricting, algorithmic governance, and quantitative fairness. The inaugural talk features CMU professor Wesley Pegden, who served as an expert witness in the Pennsylvania gerrymandering case. Pegden's team developed a probability theorem that provides statistical rigor to methods for detecting gerrymandering. The Pennsylvania Supreme Court used this mathematical evidence to rule the state's congressional districting an unconstitutional partisan gerrymander.
key_points: A new speaker series launches exploring intersections of mathematics and democratic processes
Topics span election theory, redistricting, algorithmic social infrastructure, and quantitative fairness
Wesley Pegden's mathematical theorem was used as evidence in Pennsylvania's landmark gerrymandering case
The theorem uses randomness to detect gerrymandering with statistical rigor
money_quotes: The series will host a scholarly conversation on a broad range of issues where mathematics touches on matters of democracy.
His team has proven a nice result in probability theory that adds further statistical rigor to an important new method for measuring gerrymanders.
I will discuss a theorem which allows us to use randomness to detect gerrymandering of Congressional districtings in a statistically rigorous way.
key_insight: Mathematical rigor and probability theory can provide objective, statistically sound methods for detecting partisan gerrymandering in court cases.
